brief.audience: curious adults who value evidence over sensational claims
brief.topic: How the abandoned Baychimo kept drifting through Arctic ice
brief.thesis: Baychimo's long crewless drift is documented, while the ship's final fate remains unknown.
brief.operatorNotes: Treat every route visual as approximate and do not present the ship as supernatural.

sources.title: Baychimo ship history
sources.publisher: Archives of Manitoba, Hudson's Bay Company Archives
sources.evidenceNote: Archival ship history recording the 1931 ice entrapment, disappearance in a blizzard, drift, and latest archived sighting.

sources.title: Ghost ship artifacts emerge in museum
sources.publisher: Geophysical Institute, University of Alaska Fairbanks
sources.author: Ned Rozell
sources.evidenceNote: University account based on museum research describing abandonment, later sightings, boardings, and recovered artifacts.

sources.title: Rediscovering lost relationships: Canadian Arctic ethnographic materials recovered from the ghost ship Baychimo
sources.author: Joshua D. Reuther and Jason S. Rogers
sources.evidenceNote: Peer-reviewed account of the abandoned vessel, subsequent boardings, and the route of artifacts recovered from it.

claims.text: Baychimo was trapped in Arctic ice in 1931 and disappeared from the crew's view during a blizzard.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The archival history dates the ice entrapment and records the ship disappearing in a heavy blizzard.
The university account describes the crew looking out after the storm and finding the ship gone.

claims.text: The crew believed the ship had sunk, but local hunters later saw the abandoned steamer near the Alaskan coast.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The university account records the crew's assumption and the later sighting by Inupiat hunters.

claims.text: People sighted and sometimes boarded the crewless ship in later years as it drifted with Arctic pack ice.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The university history records later sightings, boardings, and artifacts removed from the abandoned ship.
The peer-reviewed museum study documents later boardings and recovery of material from Baychimo.

claims.text: Hudson's Bay Company Archives notes 1969 as the most recent sighting in its record.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The archive sheet identifies 1969 as its most recent noted sighting.
The university account also reports a coastal sighting in 1969.

claims.text: The evidence documents an unusual crewless drift, not a supernatural ship; Baychimo's final fate remains unknown.

sourceLinks.evidenceExcerpt: The archival record documents sightings but does not establish what ultimately happened to the vessel.
The university account separates the physical drift and recovered artifacts from ghost-ship folklore.

script.title: The ship the Arctic would not keep

beats.spokenText: In 1931, a crew looked out after a blizzard and found the ship they had left in the ice was gone.
They assumed it had sunk, but local hunters soon spotted the abandoned steamer near the Alaskan coast.
Over the following years, people saw and sometimes boarded the ship without a crew as it drifted with Arctic pack ice.
beats.onScreenText: Seen and boarded without a crew

beats.spokenText: The latest company archive record dates to 1969, decades after the ship was abandoned.
The case documents an extraordinary drift, not a supernatural ghost ship. Its final fate remains unknown.
